project_overview.project_name: Sindh Integrated Health and Population Project
project_overview.project_development_objective: To improve utilization and quality of basic RMNCAH+N (Reproductive, Maternal, Newborn, Child, and Adolescent Health with Nutrition), for poor and vulnerable populations, especially women and children, in targeted areas of Sindh
project_overview.total_financing: US$200 million (SDR 155.9 million equivalent) IDA Credit, with US$30 million counterpart funding from Government of Sindh, for total project cost of US$230 million
project_overview.beneficiaries: Direct beneficiaries: catchment population of selected Government Dispensaries (GDs), especially women, girls, children, and internally displaced populations (IDPs) affected by floods in Sindh. Indirect beneficiaries: healthcare providers and managers who can influence policy makers for necessary reforms

primary_locations: Specific focus on 392 GD catchment areas (363 rural and 29 urban in Karachi slums)
geographic_scope.coverage_type: provincial with targeted focus on remote, underserved, and flood-affected areas
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: partial - Project areas include flood-affected regions (23 of 30 districts labeled as calamity-affected by 2022 monsoon floods). Remote and hard-to-reach areas with limited infrastructure. Not primarily conflict-affected but vulnerable to climate-related disasters and natural hazards

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Improving RMNCAH+N services utilization and quality and support during public health emergencies
key_components.description: Integrated care of RMNCAH+N services with coordinated patient care through network of GDs with strong referral pathways to BHUs, RHCs, THQs, and DHQs. Includes: (1.1) Public health emergency response to combat health impacts from floods through mobile health units, ambulance services, medicines, and supplies (US$30M); (1.2) Strengthening/rehabilitating health facilities through refurbishment of GDs, recruitment of female health workers (WMOs, CMWs, CHWs), training on MSDS and GBV response, electronic medical records system, and integration of FP services (US$125M); (1.3) Strengthening referral hospitals for obstetric and neonatal care through equipment, blood storage units, and provider training (US$25M)

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Strengthening demand for RMNCAH+N services, including women's empowerment for availing health services
key_components.description: Social and Behavior Change Communication (SBCC) and community outreach activities to encourage uptake of RMNCAH+N services using social marketing strategy, rebranding of GDs, women's empowerment for exercising sexual and reproductive health rights, engagement of community leaders and husbands/mothers-in-law, social accountability interventions, annual health assemblies, and social audits. Includes mid-media activities (folk dramas, street plays, fairs) and face-to-face discussions at community centers and IDP settlements

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Project Management, Monitoring and Evaluation and Research
key_components.description: Strengthening DoH and coordinating structures for project coordination and management, including financial management, procurement, PPP node, and stakeholder engagement. Supports M&E including third-party monitoring, rapid household surveys, Service Delivery Indicator Surveys, and clinical/public health research for policy information. Includes feasibility study on service delivery redesign and capacity building of DoH staff through recruitment of additional staff/consultants and training

In the event of an Eligible Crisis or Emergency, the project will contribute by providing immediate and effective response to said crisis or emergency. Currently allocated zero financing but available for reallocation if emergency is triggered

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Department of Health (DoH), Government of Sindh, in collaboration with Population Welfare Department (PWD), Government of Sindh. Project Management Unit (PMU) established at DoH headed by Program Director with support from health and population program managers, financial management and procurement staff, M&E team, and social/environment/gender specialists
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Steering Committee (PSC) chaired by Minister for Health and Population Welfare with support from Secretaries of DoH, PWD, and Department of Finance. PSC provides guidance, oversight, and accountability, reviewing progress quarterly. District coordination teams established to oversee GD functioning and provide hands-on support. Collaboration with WHO, UNFPA, UNICEF, USAID, Gates Foundation, and Islamic Development Bank. PPPs used for ambulance services, healthcare waste management, and social mobilization activities
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers (Fourth Edition, November 2020). Streamlined procurement methods including unlimited threshold for Request for Quotation, Consultants' Qualifications for consulting services, reduced bid preparation period, suspension of standstill period, Bid Securing Declaration rather than bid security, up to 40% advance payment against unconditional bank guarantee, use of UN agencies, and waiver for performance security in small contracts. Project Procurement Strategy for Development (PPSD) prepared with fit-for-purpose arrangements. Procurement conducted by DoH in collaboration with PWD. Annual procurement post-review and regular implementation support missions. External oversight by Auditor General of Pakistan, Competition Commission, Federal Investigation Agency, National Accountability Bureau, and Public Accounts Committee
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Monitoring entails tracking tools, reporting into existing health information system, and data dashboards for performance review. Day-to-day monitoring based on administrative data, physical and financial outputs, Integrated Reproductive, Maternal, Neonatal, and Child Health database, and LHW/FWW monitoring systems. PMU conducts regular supportive supervision of GD officials, data quality checks, rapid assessments and surveys on facility performance and health outcomes. Six-monthly project progress reports prepared including environment and social safeguard compliance. Baseline, midline, and end-line surveys conducted. Mid-term review to analyze overall progress and inform course correction. PPP activities managed with support from DoH PPP node with performance monitoring for compliance and adjustments

identified_risks.risk_description: While GoS demonstrates strong interest in the project, particularly in flood emergency context, impetus for broader policy reforms and decision-making may slow down in run-up to general elections. Post-flood momentum may not be sustained for deeper reforms
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Regular meetings chaired by Chief Minister Sindh with relevant departments and World Bank management. Planning and Development Department of Sindh regularly updated on project performance and emerging risks. Project prioritized as it provides relief, rehabilitation, and access to healthcare for vulnerable populations

identified_risks.risk_description: Recent floods have adversely impacted economic outlook and may delay implementation of overdue policy adjustment. Significant risks include potential worsening of external conditions, further natural disasters, slowdown or reversals in policy adjustment before elections. May lead to macroeconomic instability impeding project results achievement
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Ongoing World Bank support to structural policy reforms in fiscal and power sector areas. International Monetary Fund Extended Fund Facility program supports sound economic management and critical structural reforms. GoS expected to prioritize project as it provides relief, rehabilitation, and healthcare access to vulnerable populations

identified_risks.risk_description: DoH has contracted PPHI for PHC delivery through BHUs, but under current project will handle service provision through GDs which historically have not performed well. GDs remotely located; ensuring health supplies with trained human resources requires considerable effort. Risks of supply, rational deployment, retention and sustainability of adequately qualified front-line staff in underserved areas
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Identify and deploy health workers living close to health facilities. Provide incentives (training, allowances) for their work. Extensive supportive supervision. Capacity enhancement of healthcare providers. Phased absorption of project GDs under PPHI contract for sustainability

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Project GDs scattered across province requiring dedicated and well-informed project managers at province and district levels. CMWs and CHWs to be hired need sufficient capacity to effectively implement activities. Project GDs need financing beyond project close to continuously provide RMNCAH+N services. Risk of inadequate governance capacity at coordinating agencies including GDs to utilize assets and in-kind support
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Capacity enhancement of healthcare providers hired under project. Deputing of additional staff to strengthen project management functions. Sustainability ensured through absorbing project GDs under PPHI contract in phased manner. GoS will transition GD functioning to PPHI with sustainable financing mechanism

identified_risks.risk_description: Financial management risks relate to: (i) untimely funds flow; (ii) inadequate asset management; (iii) protection of resources from fraud and corruption; (iv) timely and reliable financial reporting. Procurement performance in recently closed Sindh Enhance Response to Reduce Stunting Project was not adequate. Coordinating agencies may lack governance capacity
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project DA established based on RFA Procedure issued by Finance Division. Strengthen internal controls using internal audit firm for periodic internal audits coupled with annual audit by Office of AGP. Procurement carried out in accordance with World Bank Procurement Regulations. Oversight by AGP, Competition Commission, Federal Investigation Agency, National Accountability Bureau, and Public Accounts Committee. Procurement clinics and familiarization sessions for complex procurements. Red flags clinics for PIU on Anti-Corruption Guidelines

identified_risks.risk_description: Environmental risks from Component 1 civil works include air emissions, noise, dust generation, waste generation (solid, medical, construction), occupational health and safety risks, use of chemicals/solvents. Blood storage units warrant infection prevention and control measures. Risk of counterfeit or expired medicines. Transportation challenges in desert districts. Social risks include lack of meaningful engagement with vulnerable groups (religious/ethnic minorities, seasonal migrants, people with disabilities) leading to exclusion, elite capture, social tensions. Digital privacy and data protection risks from electronic medical records. Possible informal settlers on GD refurbishment sites. GBV, SEA/SH risks for community groups, children, women, and healthcare workers. SBCC ineffectiveness if not culturally appropriate
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental and Social Commitment Plan and Stakeholder Engagement Plan developed. ESMF and Environmental and Health Care Waste Management Plan to be developed during implementation. Site-specific Environment and Social Safeguards Screening and Management Plans for sub-projects. Ambulance service outsourced with criteria for hazardous waste handling and safe disposal. Driver training in safe and defensive driving. Energy-efficient equipment procurement to mitigate e-waste. Robust stakeholder engagement throughout project lifecycle. Merit-based recruitment criteria for female health workers. Privacy-by-design features for digital privacy. Resettlement action plans if informal settlers found. GBV service provider mapping. SEA/SH action plan and accountability framework. Staff training on SEA/SH risks. Code of conduct for project workers. GBV training for GD health providers. SBCC materials in local languages with culturally appropriate messaging. GRM with three tiers (site, district, provincial) with mandatory female members

identified_risks.risk_description: Risk of elite capture and social tensions in selection of beneficiaries and facilities. Potential exclusion of vulnerable groups including religious and ethnic minorities, seasonal migrants, and people with disabilities. Risk of ineffective community engagement if not culturally sensitive
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Comprehensive stakeholder engagement throughout project lifecycle. Robust and transparent criteria for merit-based recruitment and beneficiary selection. Culturally appropriate communication mechanisms at all project sites. GRM accessible to diverse affected population including women, senior citizens, minorities, and vulnerable groups. Social accountability interventions, annual health assemblies, and social audits. Involvement of community leaders and influencers in SBCC activities

identified_risks.risk_description: Operational concerns may arise due to remoteness of project areas and potential security issues affecting implementation and service delivery
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Informed selection of project locations considering security context. Regular monitoring and assessment of security situation. Coordination with local authorities and community leaders

triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS 1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS 3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management
ESS 5: Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement
ESS 10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Three-tier GRM established: (1) Site level with complaints registers and grievance focal points; (2) District level grievance committee; (3) Central (provincial level) grievance committee. GRM covers project-related construction activities, trainings, selection of GDs, recruitments, provision of medical supplies, etc. Gender-responsive with mandatory inclusion of female members at all three tiers. Aligned with SEA/SH Action Plan accountability and response framework. Separate Labor GRM per ESS2 requirements. Accessible to diverse affected population including women, senior citizens, minorities, and vulnerable groups. Culturally appropriate communication mechanisms. Toll-free helpline and suggestion boxes at health facilities. Beneficiary feedback mechanism and dedicated satisfaction surveys. World Bank Grievance Redress Service (GRS) and Accountability Mechanism also available
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Project focuses on adolescents, women of reproductive age, and GBV prevention and health sector response. Addresses gender gaps in reproductive, maternal, and adolescent health rooted in gender-based discrimination and limited access to healthcare. Targets women with limited control over financial resources and limited voice in decision-making. Addresses barriers to access including limited knowledge, access to FP methods, and barriers to ANC and skilled birth attendance. Supports: (i) improved contraceptive commodity security in underserved areas; (ii) HRD and provider training on modern contraceptive methods and proper counseling; (iii) SBCC on modern contraceptive mix targeting males and adolescents; (iv) expansion of RMNCAH+N service coverage with quality improvements. Implements comprehensive gender-responsive BCC strategy with rights-based approach targeting community influencers, husbands, mothers-in-law, and community leaders. CMWs receive quality training on interpersonal communication and RMNCAH+N services. GD providers trained on FP, RMNCAH+N, GBV prevention and response, confidentiality, privacy, and attitudes towards vulnerable groups. Ambulance services provided for referral to offset costs. Measured through indicators: women receiving ANC 4 visits, births attended by skilled attendants, healthcare providers trained on FP/RMNCAH+N/GBV, GDs with stock of modern contraceptive commodities, married women counseled for FP and availing services, GBV survivors receiving clinical management
vulnerable_groups: Poor and vulnerable populations in remote and underserved areas of Sindh
Women and girls, especially those with limited education, limited voice and agency, restricted mobility
Internally displaced populations (IDPs) affected by 2022 floods
Rural populations, particularly in hard-to-reach areas (desert, delta, riverine, arid zones, hilly areas)
Urban poor living in slums (particularly in Karachi)
Populations in flood-affected districts with high poverty rates (up to 53.4% in Badin district)
Populations with limited access to sanitation facilities and clean water

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project not in primary conflict-affected area but designed with conflict sensitivity considerations. Targets vulnerable populations including IDPs from floods. Includes GBV prevention and response services recognizing that COVID-19 pandemic exacerbated violence against women. Project design incorporates social cohesion through community engagement, social accountability mechanisms, and involvement of community leaders. Stakeholder engagement plan includes consultation with diverse groups to prevent elite capture and social tensions. GRM designed to be accessible to all affected populations including minorities. Project supports health system strengthening which is critical for resilience and stability
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Project operates in context of multiple fragility factors: (1) Climate vulnerability - Sindh highly vulnerable to flooding, extreme temperatures, droughts, and sea level rise. Pakistan ranks 18th out of 191 countries for disaster risk and 8th for flooding risk. 2022 floods unprecedented, exceeding 2010 floods in scale; (2) Economic fragility - Pakistan experiencing macroeconomic instability with high inflation (projected 23% in FY23), volatile growth (averaging under 2% per capita GDP growth), and external imbalances. Floods expected to increase poverty by up to 4 percentage points, pushing 9 million into poverty; (3) Human capital deficits - High stunting (38% nationally, 45.5% in Sindh), learning poverty (75%), poor RMNCAH+N indicators with regional disparities. Maternal mortality ratio in Sindh (224 per 100,000 live births) twice the national average; (4) Institutional capacity constraints - Health facilities in remote areas lack trained human resources, medicines, equipment. Quality of patient satisfaction with public health services low at 27%. Procurement performance issues in previous projects; (5) Social fragility - Gender-based violence prevalent (1 in 5 women experienced spousal/physical/sexual/emotional violence). Limited women's agency and decision-making power. Social norms restrict access to healthcare; (6) Geographic vulnerability - 48% of Sindh population in rural areas with 37% living below poverty line. Large socio-economic disparities. Vast areas remote and hard to reach. 271 UCs identified as hard to reach in desert, delta, riverine, arid, or hilly zones
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Project areas include remote and hard-to-reach locations which may present operational challenges. Security risks mitigated through informed selection of project locations considering security context. Regular monitoring and assessment of security situation. Coordination with local authorities and community leaders. Project design incorporates flexibility to respond to security challenges through mobile health units and community health workers. CERC component available to respond to security-related health emergencies. No specific conflict-related security threats mentioned in PAD, but remoteness and limited infrastructure present operational security considerations
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: Yes - Project directly addresses internally displaced populations (IDPs) from 2022 monsoon floods. Sindh disproportionately affected with 799 of 1,739 nationwide casualties, 8,422 injured, and 1.9 million houses damaged or destroyed (83% of nationwide total). Over 4.4 million acres of agricultural land damaged and 0.8 million livestock perished. Stagnant water gave rise to skin, gastric, and mosquito-borne diseases. IDPs reported mental health challenges. Project Component 1.1 specifically supports integrated outreach healthcare and reproductive health services to flood-affected population through mobile health units, fixed/mobile health units, delivery vans, ambulance services, and surveillance systems. Component 2 includes community outreach activities targeting IDP settlements. Project provides relief, rehabilitation, and reconstruction of health infrastructure damaged by floods. Preliminary assessments confirm over 100 health facilities fully damaged and 800 partially damaged. Healthcare was inaccessible due to damaged roads, non-functional facilities, lack of human resources and medicines. Project supports emergency response to health impacts including vector-borne diseases (malaria, dengue), acute watery diarrhea, cholera, skin diseases, and malnutrition. Over 3.6 million disease cases reported from July-October 2022 in Sindh. Project also addresses longer-term needs through reconstruction of health facilities with improved resilient capacities to withstand future climate-related disasters
